Overview

ActionableEvidence applies 6 independent criteria to determine whether a Cochrane meta-analysis provides truly actionable evidence. ALL 6 criteria must pass for a verdict of ACTIONABLE; failure of any single criterion yields NOT YET.

The corpus consists of 6,229 meta-analyses from 501 Cochrane systematic reviews in the Pairwise70 dataset. Each MA is recomputed from study-level data using REML random-effects models with Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman (HKSJ) adjustment.

The 6 Criteria

1. Statistical Significance

The pooled estimate must be statistically significant at p < 0.05 with HKSJ adjustment.

HKSJ provides more conservative inference than the standard DerSimonian-Laird approach, using a t-distribution with k-1 degrees of freedom and an inflated standard error when heterogeneity is present.

2. Prediction Interval Concordance

The 95% prediction interval must not cross the null (zero on log-scale).

The PI estimates the range of true effects expected in a new, similar study. Even if the pooled estimate is significant, a PI crossing zero means the effect may not generalize. Requires k ≥ 3 to compute (Higgins-Thompson-Spiegelhalter method); MAs with k < 3 pass by default.

3. Multiverse Robustness

The conclusion must survive multiverse analysis: classification of Robust or Moderate from the FragilityAtlas.

Multiverse analysis varies the estimator, CI method, bias correction, and leave-one-out strategy. "Fragile" or "Unstable" results change direction or significance under reasonable analytic choices. FragilityAtlas covers 403 of 501 reviews; uncovered reviews receive benefit of doubt (pass).

4. Low Audit Flag Burden

Must have 0 FAIL-level flags from the MetaAudit 11-detector suite.

MetaAudit detectors include: prediction gap, model misspecification, fragility, underpowered, publication bias, small-study effect, excess significance, data integrity, study overlap, overclaiming, and certainty mismatch. WARN flags are allowed; FAIL and CRITICAL flags are not.

5. Sufficient Evidence Volume

Must have k ≥ 5 studies AND total N ≥ 500 participants.

Below these thresholds, heterogeneity estimates are unreliable (for tau-squared and I-squared) and random fluctuation dominates. The k ≥ 5 threshold aligns with common guidance for random-effects meta-analysis reliability.

6. No Publication Bias Signal

Egger's test p > 0.10 (no asymmetry detected), or k < 10 (test not applicable, passes by default).

Publication bias is assessed via the MetaAudit pub_bias module. Egger's regression test has low power below k=10, so small MAs are exempt. The threshold of p > 0.10 is conventional for this exploratory test.

Limitations

  • FragilityAtlas covers only 403 of 501 reviews (80.4%). The 98 uncovered reviews receive benefit-of-doubt for the robustness criterion, which inflates the ACTIONABLE count slightly.
  • The robustness classification from FragilityAtlas is at the review level (primary analysis), not per-MA. All MAs within a review inherit the same robustness classification.
  • Prediction intervals for k < 3 and Egger's test for k < 10 are not computable, so these criteria pass by default for small MAs. This is methodologically correct but means very small MAs face fewer hurdles.
  • The 6 criteria are binary (pass/fail). A richer framework might weight criteria by importance or allow partial credit.
  • This analysis uses recomputed effects from study-level data, which may differ from the original Cochrane analysis if different effect measures, subgroup selections, or fixed-effect models were used.

References

  • Hartung J, Knapp G (2001). A refined method for the meta-analysis of controlled clinical trials with binary outcome. Statistics in Medicine.
  • Higgins JPT, Thompson SG, Spiegelhalter DJ (2009). A re-evaluation of random-effects meta-analysis. JRSS-A.
  • IntHout J, Ioannidis JPA, Borm GF (2014). The Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman method for random effects meta-analysis is straightforward and considerably outperforms the standard DerSimonian-Laird method. BMC Medical Research Methodology.
  • Egger M et al. (1997). Bias in meta-analysis detected by a simple, graphical test. BMJ.
